title: Biostatistics for Clinical Research
description: Master the essential statistical methods for designing and analyzing clinical trials through two adaptive learning pathways

nodes.title: Welcome to Clinical Biostatistics
nodes.content: Biostatistics is the foundation of rigorous clinical research. Whether you're designing a drug trial, evaluating a diagnostic test, or interpreting study results, statistical reasoning protects patients and advances medicine. This course covers two pathways: master the statistical theory behind clinical decision-making, or jump directly into practical study design. Both paths lead to deeper understanding of how evidence shapes clinical practice.

nodes.title: Choose Your Learning Path
nodes.content: Do you prefer building a strong theoretical foundation first, or learning through practical clinical examples?

choices.label: Practical First: Learn through study design

nodes.title: Hypothesis Testing & Statistical Significance
nodes.content: Hypothesis testing is the engine of clinical research. You begin with a null hypothesis (H₀: no difference) and test whether your data provide sufficient evidence to reject it. The p-value measures this evidence: a p-value < 0.05 traditionally means you'd observe this result by chance alone less than 5% of the time if the null were true. Type I error (false positive, rejecting a true null) and Type II error (false negative, failing to reject a false null) represent the two ways hypothesis tests can mislead. A 95% confidence interval captures the range where the true effect likely resides.

nodes.title: Worked Example: Antihypertensive Drug Trial
nodes.content: A clinical trial tests whether a new antihypertensive reduces blood pressure more than placebo in 200 patients. The new drug reduces BP by 12 mmHg versus 6 mmHg for placebo (difference = 6 mmHg). The t-test yields p = 0.03, meaning if the drug had no effect, there's only a 3% chance of observing a 6 mmHg difference by random variation. The 95% confidence interval is [1, 11] mmHg. Researchers reject the null hypothesis and conclude the drug is effective. This example shows how p-values guide decision-making: since 0.03 < 0.05, the evidence favors the treatment.

nodes.title: Assessment: Statistical Foundations
nodes.content: Test your understanding of hypothesis testing, p-values, and confidence intervals in clinical research.

questions.question: A study reports p = 0.02 for a comparison between treatment groups. What does this p-value mean?
options: There is a 2% probability the treatment actually works
If the null hypothesis (no difference) were true, there is a 2% probability of observing this result or more extreme by chance alone
The treatment is definitely effective
The researcher made a 2% error in calculation
feedback: Incorrect. The p-value is not the probability the treatment works.
Correct! This is the precise definition of a p-value.
Incorrect. Statistical significance does not prove causation.
Incorrect. The p-value is not a measure of analytical error.
questions.explanation: The p-value is a conditional probability: given the null is true, how likely is our observed data? It is NOT the probability that our hypothesis is true.

questions.question: What is a Type I error in clinical research?
options: Concluding a treatment works when it actually doesn't (false positive)
Concluding a treatment doesn't work when it actually does (false negative)
Using the wrong statistical test
Enrolling too few patients
feedback: Correct! Type I error is a false positive.
Incorrect. This is a Type II error.
Incorrect. This is a methodological error, not a Type I error.
Incorrect. Small sample size increases Type II error risk.
questions.explanation: Type I error (α) occurs when you reject a true null hypothesis. We set α = 0.05 to control the false positive rate.

questions.question: A 95% confidence interval for treatment effect is [2, 8] units. Which statement is correct?
options: There is a 95% probability the true effect is between 2 and 8 units
We can be 95% confident that the true effect lies between 2 and 8 units
95% of the data falls between 2 and 8 units
The treatment has a 95% chance of being effective
feedback: Common interpretation, but technically imprecise.
Correct! This is the proper frequentist interpretation.
Incorrect. The CI describes the parameter, not the data.
Incorrect. The CI does not address treatment efficacy probability.
questions.explanation: A confidence interval is a range such that if the experiment were repeated many times, about 95% of computed intervals would contain the true parameter.

nodes.title: Study Design & Sample Size in Clinical Research
nodes.content: Before collecting data, researchers must design the study: Will it be a randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing treatment vs. control, or an observational study? Sample size determines statistical power—your ability to detect a real effect if it exists. Power depends on effect size (the smallest clinically meaningful difference), significance level (0.05), outcome variability, and sample size. Underpowered studies waste patient time; overpowered studies expose unnecessary participants to risk. Calculating sample size before starting is a hallmark of rigorous clinical research. Randomization balances confounding variables; observational studies risk bias from unmeasured confounders.

nodes.title: Real Example: COVID-19 Vaccine Efficacy Trial
nodes.content: The Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 trial aimed to detect a 30% relative risk reduction (clinically meaningful effect). Statisticians calculated they needed ~44,000 participants to achieve 90% power at α = 0.05, accounting for an expected 1% symptomatic infection rate in placebo. They randomized half to vaccine, half to placebo. The interim analysis showed 95 infections in the vaccine arm versus 162 in placebo, yielding 41% efficacy—far exceeding the 30% target. The pre-calculated sample size ensured adequate power; the actual results were substantially stronger than the minimum detectable effect.

nodes.title: Assessment: Study Design & Sample Size
nodes.content: Apply your knowledge of study design, statistical power, and sample size calculation.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT directly affect the required sample size in a clinical trial?
options: The smallest treatment effect the researcher wants to detect (effect size)
The desired statistical power (e.g., 90%)
The expected variance in outcome measurements
The nationality of the trial sponsor
feedback: Incorrect. Larger target effect size reduces required sample size.
Incorrect. Higher power requires larger sample.
Incorrect. Higher variance requires larger sample.
Correct! Sponsor nationality does not affect statistical sample size.
questions.explanation: Sample size depends on effect size, power, significance level, and outcome variability. Administrative factors like sponsorship do not change these statistical requirements.

questions.question: What does 90% statistical power mean in a clinical trial?
options: There is a 90% probability the treatment works
If the treatment has a true effect of the size specified in the sample size calculation, there is a 90% probability the study will detect it
90% of participants will respond to the treatment
The results are 90% reliable
feedback: Incorrect. Power is not the probability the treatment works.
Correct! Power is the probability of detecting a true effect of the assumed size.
Incorrect. Power describes statistical performance, not response rates.
Incorrect. Power has a specific technical definition.
questions.explanation: Power = 1 − (Type II error rate). With 90% power, if the true effect equals your assumed size, you have a 90% chance of rejecting the null hypothesis.

questions.question: Why is inferring causation harder in observational studies than in randomized controlled trials?
options: Observational studies always have smaller sample sizes
Patients choose their treatment, so outcome differences may reflect differences in patient types (confounding) rather than treatment effects
Outcome measurement is always less accurate in observational studies
Observational studies cannot calculate effect sizes
feedback: Incorrect. Observational studies can be very large.
Correct! Confounding by indication and selection bias are major threats to causal inference.
Incorrect. Accuracy depends on the outcome, not study type.
Incorrect. Effect size can be calculated in any study.
questions.explanation: Randomization balances potential confounders between groups. In observational studies, patients self-select, so sicker patients might choose one treatment over another, biasing the comparison.

nodes.title: Clinical Biostatistics Mastery Achieved
nodes.content: You've mastered the core principles of clinical biostatistics: hypothesis testing, p-values, Type I and II errors, confidence intervals, study design, and sample size calculation. These tools enable you to design sound clinical studies, interpret results critically, and contribute to evidence-based medicine. Continue deepening your expertise by reading clinical trial papers and asking: How was sample size calculated? Are results statistically and clinically significant? What could confound the findings? With these questions, you'll advance the quality of clinical research you touch.
